rulings.Case Title: RALPH OLIVERIO VS. TRANSDEV SERVICES, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Transdev Services Inc.'S Notice Of Motion And Motion For Order Overruling Defendant'S Demurrer As To Plaintiff'S Unfair Competition Claim; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities In Support
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Friday, May 25, 2018, Line 2, DEFENDANT TRANSDEV SERVICES, INC.'S Motion For Order Overruling Defendant'S Demurrer As To Plaintiff'S Unfair Competition Claim. Defendant Transdev Services, Inc.'s "motion for an order overruling demurrer to plaintiff's unfair competition claim" is DENIED. Transdev's demurrer has already been overruled by the Court of Appeal's December 22, 2017 decision, which called for no further demurrer order by the trial court, but simply "further proceedings." (See, e.g., Aroney v. California Horse Racing Board (1983) 145 Cal.App.3d 928, 933 (on remand, trial court instructed to "vacate its order sustaining the demurrer and to enter a new and different order overruling the demurrer").) Transdev is ordered to answer the complaint on or before June 4, 2018. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for plaintiff is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/RBU)

rulings.Case Title: ANDREW PILLET VS. STEPHEN H KENDRICK ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel The Following: 1. Answers To Second Set Of Form Interrogatories [Form #2] [Claire E. Kendrick, Rosemary H. Kendrick, And Katharine A. rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Friday, May 25, 2018, Line 1, PLAINTIFF ANDREW PILLET's Motion To Compel The Following: 1. Answers To Second Set Of Form Interrogatories [Form #2] [Claire E. Kendrick, Rosemary H. Kendrick, And Katharine A. Kendrick], 2. Monetary Sanctions Directed Against Claire E. Kendrick, Rosemary H. Kendrick, And Katharine A. Kendrick For The Failure And Refusal To Respond To Form #2. Pro Tem Judge James Fleming, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Off calendar per request of the moving party. (email request 5/18/18). Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to jameshfleming@gmail.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested.=(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: YEVGEN TIZENBERG VS. CHAIRMAN SF, LLC ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Motion To Set Aside Dismissal Pursuant To Ccp 473(B)
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Friday, May 25, 2018, Line 3, PLAINTIFF YEVGEN TIZENBERG'S Motion To Set Aside Dismissal Pursuant To Ccp 473(B) Plaintiff's motion to set aside dismissal pursuant to CCP 473(b) is GRANTED. The mandatory relief of CCP 473(b) does not require explanation of an attorney's "mistake, inadvertence, surprise or excusable neglect." (Martin Potts & Assoc., Inc. v. Corsair LLC (2016) 244 Cal.App.4th 432, 440.) It is the application for relief that must be made within six months. (CCP 473(b).) Defendant may file a motion for "reasonable compensatory legal fees and costs." (Id.) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for plaintiff is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/RBU)

rulings.Case Title: SAFEDOCS, INC. A CALIFORNIA CORPORATION ET AL VS. SWETT & CRAWFORD A CALIFORNIA CORPORATION
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Swett And Crawfords Notice Of Motion And Motion For Summary Judgment Or, In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Friday, May 25, 2018, Line 4, DEFENDANT SWETT & CRAWFORD'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Motion For Summary Judgment Or, In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication. Defendant Swett & Crawford's ("Swett's) motion for summary judgment is DENIED. Swett's motion for summary adjudication is GRANTED in part and DENIED in part. The motion is GRANTED as to the breach of contract cause of action. " 'A contract implied in fact is one not expressed by the parties, but implied from facts and circumstances showing a mutual intention to contract.' [citation] Without such mutual intention there can be no contract in fact; hence the assumption, intention or expectation of either party alone, not made known to the other, can give rise to no inference of an implied contract in accordance therewith." (Gold v. Gibbons (1960) 178 Cal.App.2d 517, 520.) Here, the evidence shows that Safedocs and Swett never communicated with each other and Sinclair was acting as Safedocs' broker. (Swett's Undisputed Material Facts 2 and 3.) Safedocs presents no evidence of privity or that both parties intended to create a contract. (See 10 CCR 2189.2(c) and (d).) The motion is DENIED as to the professional negligence cause of action. Privity is not necessary to create a cause of action for professional negligence. (See Business to Business Markets, Inc. v. Zurich Specialties (2005) 135 Cal.App.4th 165.) Swett fails to maintain its summary adjudication burden. Appendix 38 is an e-mail from Thrasher of Swett to CFC. Swett requested the D&O coverage with the enhancements. What was delivered, however, lacked the enhancements and the enhancements only came about months later when the Amendatory Clause was issued. A reasonable trier of fact could find that Swett assumed the duty to obtain insurance for Safedocs with the enhancements in a timely fashion but followed through in a negligent manner. The court rejects Swett's argument regarding damages. The amount of Safedocs' damages is unclear and Swett admits that the amount of the Lloyd's settlement is undisclosed. (Swett's Undisputed Material Fact 22.) The court DENIES Safedocs' request for a continuance pursuant to CCP 437c(h). The Pinedo declaration does not highlight facts or explain how the outstanding discovery could create a triable issue of material fact on the breach of contract claim. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Swett is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/RBU)

rulings.Case Title: PO CHU LUI VS. KA FAI KWONG ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Notice Of Motion And Motion For Summary Judgment
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Friday, May 25, 2018, Line 5, DEFENDANTS JIA KUANG, AI WU, CHEF WANG MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT. Defendants and cross-complainants Jia Kuang's, Ai Wu's and Chef Wang's summary judgment motion is DENIED. The motion is procedurally improper in seeking to adjudicate less than all claims of the first amended complaint and/or the cross-complaint without moving for summary adjudication in the alternative. (See CCP §437c(a),(f)(1); CRC 3.1350(b).) In any event, plaintiff raises triable issues of fact as to the amount of principal loaned. (Lui Dep. pp. 174-75; Lui Dec. 4-6.) This would preclude summary adjudication of the breach of contract and common count claims in the FAC as well as the cross-complaint's usury claim. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for plaintiff is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/RBU)

rulings.Case Title: DON TUCKER ET AL VS. THE TRAVELERS INDEMNITY COMPANY OF CONNECTICUT ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant The Travelers Indemnity Company Of Connecticut'S Motion To Compel Plaintiff Don Tucker Dba Don Tucker And Son To Produce Additional Documents In Response To Travelers Second Production Requests.(For Tentative Ruling Only)
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Friday, May 25, 2018, Line 4, Defendant The Travelers Indemnity Company Of Connecticut'S Motion To Compel Plaintiff Don Tucker Dba Don Tucker And Son To Produce Additional Documents In Response To Travelers Second Production Requests.(For Tentative Ruling Only)(part 2 of 3) It follows that Mr. Tucker's ability to defend the underlying action is indeed an issue in this case. Given the acknowledged broad scope of permissible discovery, Defendant is entitled to conduct discovery of its theory of the case unless precluded by an appropriate objection. Mr. Tucker has asserted both general privacy objections and specific ones based on the so-called taxpayer's privilege. However, Mr. Tucker acknowledges that privacy rights may be overcome by the demonstration of a compelling state interest. Britt v. Superior Court, 20 Cal. 3d 844, 855 (1978). Given that the adequacy of Mr. Tucker's financial resources are placed in issue by the nature of his claim and the allegations of the Complaint, the ascertainment of the truth thereof is essential to a just resolution of this lawsuit, which constitutes a compelling state interest. As to the claimed taxpayer privilege, the same is conditional and must give way when, as here, the gravamen of the lawsuit is inconsistent with the privilege and when, again as here, a greater public policy than confidentiality of tax returns is involved, Schnabel v. Superior Court, 5 Cal. 4th 704, 721 (1993). The conclusion that Mr. Tucker's conditional privacy rights must yield to Defendant's right to discover the truth of Plaintiff's allegations further supported by the course of the meet and confer discussions wherein Mr. Tucker made a supplemental response to Request No. 29 agreeing to produce responsive documents concerning his claimed financial inability to defend himself. Despite this concession and apparent waiver, Mr. Tucker has produced only payroll records and copies of Schedule C to his tax returns, which disclose only business income and expense. These items are insufficient to disclose Mr. Tucker's total assets and his income from other sources and thus to permit a forensic conclusion as to his ability to pay the costs of a defense. To the extent that a weighing of interests is required, the Court finds that the interest in disclosure and ascertainment of the truth of disputed matters in litigation outweighs Mr. Tucker's interest in financial privacy. This conclusion is supported by the protective order previously entered in this case which apparently safeguards Mr. Tucker against embarrassing disclosures. Finally, Mr. Tucker has asserted that the discovery requests are overbroad. However, Tucker's arguments as to the breadth of the discovery requests lose force in the face of his own insufficient disclosure of his financial position despite his meet and confer agreement to do so. Moreover, Mr. Tucker has not identified any particular overreach of the requests nor requested any specific narrowing of the requests which the Court could order. Under the circumstances and the significance of the case, the acknowledged scope of permissible discovery mandates that the overbreadth objections must be overruled. (end of part 2 of 3) =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: DON TUCKER ET AL VS. THE TRAVELERS INDEMNITY COMPANY OF CONNECTICUT ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Plaintiff Don Tucker Dba Don Tucker And Son To Produce Additional Documents In Response To Travelers Second Production Requests
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Friday, May 25, 2018, Line 4, DEFENDANT THE TRAVELERS INDEMNITY COMPANY OF CONNECTICUT's Motion To Compel Plaintiff Don Tucker Dba Don Tucker And Son To Produce Additional Documents In Response To Travelers Second Production Requests. (part 1 of 3) Pro Tem Judge James Fleming, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: The motion of Defendant Travelers Indemnity Company to compel production of additional documents seeks financial documents bearing on the financial ability of Plaintiff Don Tucker to pay for his defense in the underlying personal injury lawsuit which Defendant refused to defend under a Commercial General Liability Insurance Policy issued to Plaintiff. Defendant's theory of the case is that Plaintiff may recover damages for refusal to defend only if the refusal to defend was tortious and Mr. Tucker was unable to pay for a defense in the underlying action and that Mr. Tucker is further obliged to mitigate his damages arising from Defendant's allegedly wrongful refusal to defend. Mr. Tucker has asserted in his complaint a claim of financial inability to mount a defense and has entered into a settlement agreement predicated upon such inability, both of which raise the claim of financial inability as a potentially discoverable issue. Defendant challenges the claim of financial inability and supports that challenge with, among other things, Mr. Tucker's deposition testimony that he purchased a $300,000 automobile for cash within a few weeks of entering into the settlement agreement, leading to a default trial and judgment against Mr. Tucker for $18,500,000. Mr. Tucker also testified that he had between $300,000 and $600,000 in his checking account when he bought the car. Mr. Tucker's testimony further supports that he did not want to spend his own funds to defend the underlying case and did not feel he should have to do so, and not because of any lack of financial wherewithal. Mr. Tucker testified that he did not know his financial ability or how much a defense might cost. For the law applicable to the consequences of an insurer's refusal to defend, both parties rely upon Amato v. Mercury Cas. Co., 53 Cal. App. 4th 825 (1997), albeit for different purposes. As relevant here, Amato holds that "where an insurer tortiously breaches the duty to defend and the insured suffers a default judgment because the insured is unable to defend, the insurer is liable for the default judgment, which is a proximate result of its wrongful refusal to defend." (end of part 1 of 3) =(302/JPT)

Counsel for plaintiffs is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/RBU)
